definition: A tissue-resident macrophage that localizes adjacent to blood vessels across multiple non-CNS tissues including dermis, lung, heart, and adipose tissue. In mice, this population is characterised by high expression of Lyve1, Folr2, and Stab1, low surface MHCII and low CX3CR1, combined with CD206 and F4/80 expression (Chakarov et al. 2019, DOI:10.1126/science.aau0964). It promotes sprouting angiogenesis and blood vessel morphogenesis (Gopee et al. 2024, DOI:10.1038/s41586-024-08002-x) and regulates arterial tone through hyaluronan-mediated control of smooth muscle cell collagen deposition in mice (Lim et al. 2018, DOI:10.1016/j.immuni.2018.06.008). A paired MHCII-high interstitial macrophage occupies a distinct non-perivascular niche in the same tissues (Dick et al. 2022, DOI:10.1126/science.abf7777).

logical_axioms.notes: Documented in prenatal human skin (Gopee et al. 2024); the population is cross-tissue so CL editors may prefer a broader anatomical term or separate tissue-specific subclasses.
LYVE1++ macrophages express pro-angiogenic gene programs and ligand-receptor interactions (CXCL8-ACKR1, CCL8-ACKR1) that promote sprouting angiogenesis in prenatal skin (Gopee et al. 2024).
Gene module analysis identifies blood vessel morphogenesis as the principal functional signature of LYVE1++ macrophages in prenatal skin (Gopee et al. 2024).

Perivascular macrophages of this class regulate arterial tone through hyaluronan-LYVE1 signalling controlling smooth muscle cell collagen in mice; Lyve1-Cre depletion causes arterial stiffening and impaired blood flow (Lim et al. 2018).

logical_axioms.filler: lymphatic vessel endothelial hyaluronic acid receptor 1
logical_axioms.notes: High LYVE1 surface expression is the principal marker distinguishing this population from the paired MHCII-high interstitial macrophage across all tissues studied (Chakarov et al. 2019; Dick et al. 2022).

synonyms.value: LYVE1 hi MHCII lo interstitial macrophage

references.supports: Cross-tissue identification of Lyve1hi MHCIIlo CX3CR1lo CD206+ F4/80+ Folr2+/Stab1+ perivascular interstitial macrophage population in mouse dermis, lung, heart, and adipose tissue; Slco2b1-DTR depletion confirms perivascular niche

LYVE-1-expressing perivascular macrophages regulate arterial tone through hyaluronan-mediated control of smooth muscle cell collagen; Lyve1-Cre depletion in mice causes arterial stiffening and collagen deposition
Three-tissue conservation of Lyve1hi vs MHCIIhi paired interstitial macrophage populations (heart, gut, dermis); fate-mapping distinguishes the two spatially distinct niches

LYVE1++ macrophage identification in prenatal human skin 7-17 PCW; perivascular co-localisation confirmed by Visium spatial transcriptomics and multiplex RNAscope/immunofluorescence; pro-angiogenic gene module and ligand-receptor interactions with endothelial cells; fibroblast maintenance via IGF1-IGF1R and GRN-EGFR signalling
Cross-organ developmental immune atlas used to annotate LYVE1++ macrophage subset in prenatal skin; LYVE1hi macrophages enriched in early gestation with high self-renewal potential
Adult skin perivascular macrophages of this class are CCR2-negative, CX3CR1-negative/low, CD206-high, LYVE1-high; their depletion impairs angiogenesis and skin repair

justification: No CL term exists for the LYVE1-high, MHCII-low perivascular interstitial macrophage that is conserved across multiple non-CNS tissues (dermis, lung, heart, adipose). The nearest candidate, CL:0000881 'perivascular macrophage', is explicitly restricted by its CL definition to CNS brain vasculature ('adjacent to a small blood vessel of a brain') and therefore cannot be applied to skin or other peripheral tissues. The border associated macrophage term (CL:4042003) is similarly CNS-specific. The broader parent CL:0000864 'tissue-resident macrophage' captures the tissue-resident nature but not the perivascular niche identity, the LYVE1hi/MHCIIlo immunophenotype, or the pro-angiogenic/vascular homeostasis functions. This population forms a conserved cross-tissue pair with a MHCII-high interstitial macrophage counterpart that occupies a distinct non-perivascular niche in the same tissues (Dick et al. 2022, DOI:10.1126/science.abf7777). A new term for each paired population would enable precise cross-tissue, cross-species annotation.
ntr_markdown:
**Preferred term label**
perivascular tissue-resident macrophage

**Synonyms** (add reference(s), please)
LYVE1-positive interstitial macrophage (exact) — DOI:10.1126/science.aau0964
LYVE1-high macrophage (exact) — DOI:10.1126/science.aau0964
LYVE1 hi MHCII lo interstitial macrophage (exact) — DOI:10.1126/science.aau0964
LYVE1+ macrophage (exact) — DOI:10.1038/s41586-024-08002-x
LYVE1++ macrophage (related) — DOI:10.1038/s41586-024-08002-x

**Definition** (free text, with reference(s), please. PubMed ID format is PMID:XXXXXX)
A tissue-resident macrophage that localizes adjacent to blood vessels across multiple non-CNS tissues including dermis, lung, heart, and adipose tissue. In mice, this population is characterised by high expression of Lyve1, Folr2, and Stab1, low surface MHCII and low CX3CR1, combined with CD206 and F4/80 expression (Chakarov et al. 2019, DOI:10.1126/science.aau0964). It promotes sprouting angiogenesis and blood vessel morphogenesis (Gopee et al. 2024, DOI:10.1038/s41586-024-08002-x) and regulates arterial tone through hyaluronan-mediated control of smooth muscle cell collagen deposition in mice (Lim et al. 2018, DOI:10.1016/j.immuni.2018.06.008). A paired MHCII-high interstitial macrophage occupies a distinct non-perivascular niche in the same tissues (Dick et al. 2022, DOI:10.1126/science.abf7777).

**Parent cell type term** (check the hierarchy here https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ols4/ontologies/cl)
tissue-resident macrophage (CL:0000864) — https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ols4/ontologies/cl/classes/http%253A%252F%252Fpurl.obolibrary.org%252Fobo%252FCL_0000864

**Anatomical structure where the cell type is found** (check Uberon for anatomical structures: https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ols4/ontologies/uberon)
dermis (UBERON:0002067) — https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ols4/ontologies/uberon/classes/http%253A%252F%252Fpurl.obolibrary.org%252Fobo%252FUBERON_0002067

Note: this population is cross-tissue (also documented in lung, heart, adipose tissue, and gut) and is described here in the context of prenatal human skin (Gopee et al. 2024). Dermis is the primary atlas-derived location; CL editors may wish to use a broader anatomical parent or add tissue-specific subclasses.

**Your ORCID**
[To be added by submitter]

**Additional notes or concerns**
This term is needed because no CL term covers the LYVE1-high, MHCII-low perivascular interstitial macrophage conserved across multiple non-CNS tissues.

The nearest existing candidate, CL:0000881 'perivascular macrophage', is explicitly restricted by its CL definition to the CNS ('adjacent to a small blood vessel of a brain') and cannot be applied to skin or other peripheral tissues. CL:4042003 'border associated macrophage' is also CNS-specific. CL:0000864 'tissue-resident macrophage' is the correct broad parent but lacks the perivascular niche identity, LYVE1hi/MHCIIlo immunophenotype, and pro-angiogenic/vascular homeostasis functional profile.

This population forms a conserved cross-tissue pair with a MHCII-high interstitial macrophage counterpart; the two populations share the same tissue but occupy spatially distinct niches (Dick et al. 2022, DOI:10.1126/science.abf7777). New terms for both paired populations would be valuable for cross-tissue annotation.

Proposed logical axioms:
- subClassOf 'part of' some 'dermis' (UBERON:0002067)
- subClassOf 'capable of part of' some 'sprouting angiogenesis' (GO:0002040)
- subClassOf 'capable of part of' some 'blood vessel morphogenesis' (GO:0048514)
- subClassOf 'capable of part of' some 'blood vessel diameter maintenance' (GO:0097746)
- subClassOf 'has high plasma membrane amount' some 'lymphatic vessel endothelial hyaluronic acid receptor 1' (PR:000010012)

Key references:
- Chakarov et al. (2019) — DOI:10.1126/science.aau0964 — supports: cross-tissue Lyve1hi MHCIIlo CX3CR1lo CD206+ F4/80+ Folr2+/Stab1+ perivascular IM identity and mouse marker profile
- Lim et al. (2018) — DOI:10.1016/j.immuni.2018.06.008 — supports: vascular homeostasis function; Lyve1-Cre depletion causes arterial stiffening in mice
- Dick et al. (2022) — DOI:10.1126/science.abf7777 — supports: three-tissue conservation; paired MHCIIhi vs Lyve1hi IM fate-mapping across heart, gut, dermis
- Gopee et al. (2024) — DOI:10.1038/s41586-024-08002-x — supports: prenatal human skin perivascular localisation, pro-angiogenic function, spatial transcriptomics
- Suo et al. (2022) — DOI:10.1126/science.abo0510 — supports: cross-organ developmental atlas annotation context
- Sim et al. (2022) — DOI:10.3390/biom12111659 — supports: adult skin CCR2-/CD206+ LYVE1+ phenotype
